questions.question: What does the threshold theorem guarantee?

options.text: Quantum computers will always be faster than classical
Below a threshold error rate, arbitrarily accurate quantum computation is possible
All quantum codes have the same threshold
Physical error rates will eventually reach zero

questions.explanation: The threshold theorem guarantees that if the physical error rate p is below a critical threshold p_th, then arbitrarily long quantum computations can be performed with arbitrarily low logical error rate, using only polynomial overhead.

questions.question: How does the overhead scale with target error rate ε in the threshold theorem?
questions.explanation: The overhead is polylogarithmic in 1/ε. This means achieving 10× better accuracy requires only a small multiplicative increase in resources, not an exponential increase. This is what makes fault-tolerant QC feasible.

questions.question: What happens when the physical error rate is above the threshold?

options.text: Error correction still works but more slowly
Larger codes make the logical error rate worse

questions.explanation: Above threshold, increasing the code distance makes the logical error rate worse, not better. The error correction overhead introduces more errors than it fixes, and p_L approaches 0.5 (random guessing) as d increases.

questions.question: In the concatenation proof, how does the error rate decrease with each level?
questions.explanation: Each level of concatenation with a distance-3 code squares the effective error rate: p_L = c·p². This quadratic reduction, when iterated, gives doubly exponential suppression: after k levels, error is ~ (c·p)^(2^k).

questions.question: Why do circuit-level noise models give lower thresholds than code capacity models?

options.text: Circuit models include errors from syndrome measurement gates
Code capacity models include more error types

questions.explanation: Circuit-level models include errors from all gate locations: single-qubit gates, two-qubit gates, state preparation, and measurement. Code capacity models assume perfect syndrome extraction, which is unrealistic. Real circuits have more error sources.

questions.question: What is the approximate circuit-level threshold for the surface code?
questions.explanation: The surface code has a circuit-level threshold of approximately 0.8-1.1%, depending on the exact noise model and decoder. This is remarkably high compared to other codes and is one of the main reasons surface codes are popular.

questions.question: The threshold theorem proves that quantum error correction works for any type of noise, including correlated errors.
questions.explanation: False. The threshold theorem assumes specific noise models (typically independent, identically distributed errors). Correlated errors, non-Markovian noise, or leakage can violate the theorem's assumptions and may require different techniques.
